entries.title: US ties to Israel rattle rare earth deal in Malaysia
entries.summary: Malaysia's rare earth refinery in Gebeng is essential to a strategic deal between the Pentagon and Australia's Lynas mining corporation. Now, Malaysian activists are using it as leverage to support Palestinians in Gaza.
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

Malaysia's rare earth refinery in Gebeng is essential to a strategic deal between the Pentagon and Australia's Lynas mining corporation. Now, Malaysian activists are using it as leverage to support Palestinians in Gaza.

The US needs to secure a supply of rare earth elements independent of China — but Washington's Middle East policy is now triggering pushback on a $96 million (€84 million) rare earth deal in Muslim-majority Malaysia.

In March, the US Department of Defense (DoD) signed a four-year deal with Australian mining firm Lynas to obtain 17 metals known as rare earths. These elements are at the core of modern industrial production and used for everything from smartphones and electric cars to cutting-edge weapons systems.

The strategic deal, however, hinges on Lynas' main processing plant in Gebeng, a gritty industrial town hugging the east coast of mainland Malaysia — the largest facility of its kind outside of China.

Malaysia's government pressured over US-Israel ties

The news of the deal has prompted strong backlash in Malaysia, which has a long record of championing Palestinian statehood and rebuking Israel over its actions in Gaza and the West Bank.

Locals are urging the government in Kuala Lumpur to either block the Lynas deal or provide guarantees that the rare earths that pass through the Gebeng plant would not end up in any weapons system.

In an open letter to Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim, over 50 non-government groups said the deal risks implicating the country in "credible allegations of violations of international law across multiple theaters" including Gaza, pointing out that the US is one of Israel's main arms suppliers.

The connection between Malaysian rare earths and Israeli weapons, however, is less than straightforward. While the materials can be used to build fighter jets and missiles, they could also end up in washing machines or wind turbines.

"What the Malaysian protesters demand is the equivalent of banning natural rubber exports to the US because the tires might end up on Israeli vehicles in Gaza," Thomas Kruemmer, director of Ginger International Trade and Investment, a Singapore-based firm focused on the rare earths market, told DW.

Lynas key to providing dysprosium and terbium

A parliamentary committee held a hearing last week to determine if the Lynas project would jeopardize "Malaysia's reputation as a staunch supporter of Palestine." Anwar's government now has until the end of the month to state its official position on the deal.

"For the Malaysians, this isn't really a case about rare earths. It's a case about Palestine," Kruemmer said,

The activists hope to use the Gebeng refinery as leverage affecting US geopolitical calculations. The US' rival China currently does most of the world's mining and processing of the metals. Beijing has also been restricting exports of these materials to the West.

Moreover, Australia's Lynas is one of the few companies that can provide commercial quantities of two particular rare earths: dysprosium and terbium, according to Kruemmer. He notes that America's flagship rare earths investment project, MP Materials' Mountain Pass mine in California, has little of either.

"And since China stops rare earth exports destined for military applications, Lynas will be the only source available to the Pentagon for the time being," said Kruemmer.

Rare earths 'national security imperative' for US

With few rare earth sourcing options outside of China at the moment, the US government is investing in several projects both at home and abroad. But these can take years to come online, says Meredith Schwartz, an associate fellow at the Center for Strategic and International Studies.

"The Pentagon needs secure supply in the event China continues to deny access — it is a national security imperative. This makes projects that are actively operating, like Lynas' rare earth refinery in Malaysia, especially strategic to the United States," she said.

Australia's rich mineral deposits and mining expertise make it "central" to Washington's plans to break China's near-monopoly on rare earths, Schwartz added. She also called the Lynas refinery in Malaysia, which gets its raw material from Australia, a "key node" in the "mine to magnet supply chain."

Pentagon's involvement indicates wartime use

The deal announcement published by Lynas in March did not say whether any of the material it sells to the US Department of Defense will be used for weapons.

But given that the deal is specifically with the Department of Defense, says Kruemmer, it probably will.

"If the material weren't destined for defense, the Pentagon wouldn't have signed the contract — it doesn't care about Ford Motor's EVs or Whirlpool's kitchen gear," he said.

Neither Lynas nor the DoD replied to DW's requests for comment.

Pollution trauma haunts Malaysia

Foreign miners have a practical need to gain the trust of the local community for their operations, says Schwartz.

That is a tall order for rare earth projects in Malaysia, where a Japanese-backed venture in the 1980s left behind pools of radioactive waste — an inherent byproduct of mining and refining the metals. These have been blamed for a spate of birth defects, cancer cases and deaths in the region.

Lynas has also been hounded by questions over its own handling of radioactive waste.

If the Malaysian government were to try and block or at least "frustrate" the US-Lynas deal, it could possibly raise those concerns again and connect them to license renewals for the Gebeng plant, says Oh Ei Sun, a senior fellow at the Singapore Institute of International Affairs.

International law can also be used as a point of pressure. In their letter to Prime Minister Anwar, groups of activists cited Malaysia's obligation to ensure that anyone in its jurisdiction also respects this broader legal framework, he added.

"It's basically political will. If you want to do something, you can always find an excuse for doing that," Oh said.

Will Anwar's government risk isolation?

Whether the government will actually want to disrupt the project is another question.

"As of this moment, it [Malaysia] is still a major trading power, a major investment destination in the world," said Oh, and "once you do such things, you are going to isolate yourself, and that's a choice the Malaysian government has to make."

At the same time, Malaysia is also an increasingly conservative country. Islamist parties are on the rise and the government is facing pressure to appease the Muslim majority.

Anwar's government could score some political points by moving against the US-Lynas deal, says Oh.

"So, if I'm a politician and political survival is my main goal, I could do something to buttress my religious credentials, and the Palestinian cause is a very convenient and handy way of doing so."

Edited by: Darko Janjevic

entries.title: Spain won the World Cup, but Donald Trump 'cast a shadow'
entries.summary: Though Donald Trump mostly stayed clear of stadiums, the World Cup may influence the US midterm elections. DW looks at the political implications of "the most successful sporting event maybe in the history of the world."
entries.content:
United States of America

Spain won the World Cup, but Donald Trump 'cast a shadow'

July 20, 2026

Though Donald Trump mostly stayed clear of stadiums, the World Cup may influence the US midterm elections. DW looks at the political implications of "the most successful sporting event maybe in the history of the world."

Rodri, like anyone who has ever played football, had dreamed of this moment. The Spain captain held the World Cup in his hands, surrounded by jubilant teammates. But, before he could lift the trophy, he had to find a way to politely usher the world's most powerful man off the stage.

He didn't quite succeed. In the end, it required FIFA President Gianni Infantino to jog in front of the players to try and move Donald Trump on. Still the US president lingered next to Barcelona midfielder Pedri, bathing in the adulation he had been denied when walking on to boos with Infantino moments earlier. Trump had overstayed his welcome in similar fashion at the Club World Cup last year.

Though Sunday's final, in which Spain beat Argentina 1-0, was the first match of the tournament that Trump attended, he declared it "the most successful sporting event maybe in the history of the world" and was a constant presence even in his absence.

"Trump cast a shadow over the entire tournament, weaponizing it for domestic political purposes, regardless of whether he showed up at matches in person," Jules Boykoff, a professor in the Politics and Government Department at Pacific University in the US state of Oregon, told DW.

Trump's 'notorious' intervention

Trump administration policies denied entry to the United States for a Somali FIFA referee, forced Iran to train in Mexico and travel to games in Los Angeles and Seattle, and barred fans from around the world from attending matches. On top of all that, Boykoff said, Trump's knockout-stage phone call to FIFA "will most assuredly go down as one of the most notorious political interventions in World Cup history."

Though Trump' successfully lobbied to overturn the red card suspension of star striker Folarin Balogun, the strategy backfired on the field, with the US losing its last-16 match against a fired-up Belgium 4-1. "I knew it was going to cause a lot of controversy," Balogun said. "I could almost see within my teammates a bit of nerves because it was something that's so unique."

Boykoff said the sporting implications would not have been at the forefront of Trump's mind. "Intervening in the red card situation allowed him to deliver the message to his political base that he could get international bodies like FIFA to bend to his will," said Boykoff, who represented the US at the youth level as a player. "Most of Trump's actions during the World Cup should be read as efforts to influence a domestic political audience ahead of a key midterm election, not as an attempt to convince an international audience that he's some great guy."

Trump insisted that the tournament has boosted the US's international reputation. "All over the world, people have come and they love our country," he told reporters.

Was Balogun call a mistake for Trump?

The US midterm elections are set for November 3 and, with the war in Iran ongoing, Trump's approval rating is below 40%. Though a "shakedown" of a global organization such as FIFA may have an appeal with a base he's already won over, Karim Zidan told DW that the Balogun intervention was a political misstep for Trump.

"I think that, in boasting about this when the United States men's national team gave an opportunity for him to rally behind a team that seemed to be unifying Americans, he managed to find a way to taint it, and those who dislike him hated him just that little bit more," Zidan said.

Such unprecedented and direct political intervention in a footballing decision drew scorn and fury from most of the rest of the world — with politicians, football federations, fans and coaches all piling on to criticize. But that won't bother Trump, Boykoff said.

"Based on his actions, Trump does not appear to care much about what the rest of the world thinks of him. His anti-immigration policies stained the World Cup and undercut FIFA's slogan that 'Football Unites the World.' Trump used the tournament to further fracture the world while stoking his base's worst instincts ahead of the midterm elections," he said.

Sports-shaping as US strategy

The Balogun incident was Trump's first real foray into the tournament and came over three weeks after it had begun. Given how present he was in the buildup, that came as a surprise to some. But Zidan said the geopolitical situation had been keeping him busy, while the 250th US independence day also occupied Trump's time during the tournament. 

"All he's really interested in is making this about himself or reaping any sort of personal prestige from an event," Zidan said. "So I'm not really surprised at all that he wasn't very present at standard World Cup games."

Despite his apparent lack of interest, Trump made a play for future World Cups immediately after the 2026 tournament ended. "I had a great idea for Gianni: You have to do two countries. Announce us again next time and then announce another country after that. It'll take out some of the anger and the shock," he told Fox Sports. "Based on the numbers, we will be requesting it again immediately."

The earliest the US could host the men's World Cup again is 2038, but Los Angeles will host the Summer Olympics in 2028, with Trump still in power ahead of the presidential election that November. The US is hosting the 2034 Winter Olympics in in Salt Lake City and is likely to win the Women's World Cup for 2031. 

UFC a safe sporting haven for Trump

Zidan thinks that Trump will first retreat to the safety of the mixed-martial-arts Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC), which he managed to include in the celebrations of the 250th anniversary of the United States.

"Whenever he has shown up at a sports event that is not a UFC event, he's received resounding boos. It's not the massive warm welcome he is used to receiving at the UFC," said Zidan, whose latest book, "The Ultimate Strongmen," focuses on Trump's relationship with the UFC.

"That's why he shows up so often there, why he's hosted a UFC event at the White House. He understands that is his perfect audience and his safe space, and, no matter how poorly he's performing and no matter what he's doing in the world, no matter if there's a war going on, he will be welcomed."

Though FIFA and its president rolled out the red carpet for Trump and the 2026 World Cup made more profit than any tournament before, it is the US voters, not the executive bodies of football organizations, whom Trump must impress now. Whichever way the midterms go, it's unlikely he'll be ushered off the political stage anytime soon.

Edited by: Milan Gagnon

entries.title: Why Japan's new imperial law keeps women off the throne
entries.summary: New legislation aims to address the shrinking ranks of Japan's imperial family, while preserving male-only succession despite broad support for a female emperor.
entries.content:
Japan

Why Japan's new imperial law keeps women off the throne

July 20, 2026

New legislation aims to address the shrinking ranks of Japan's imperial family, while preserving male-only succession despite broad support for a female emperor.

Japan's Parliament on Friday approved the first major revision of the country's imperial succession laws, to address the risk that the last young male heir may not have a son.

By law, only a paternal-lineage male can become emperor in Japan. Female royals have been required to leave the imperial family when they marry commoners.

Japanese Emperor Naruhito, who acceded to the Imperial Throne in 2019, and his wife Empress Masako have one child, 24-year-old Princess Aiko, who was born in 2021. She cannot inherit the throne under the current system.

Instead, Naruhito's younger brother Prince Akishino (also known as Fumihito) is considered the crown prince. Akishino's son, Prince Hisahito, would be second in line to the throne.

In 2005, a panel appointed by then-Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi unanimously recommended allowing female emperors and succession through the female line. The proposal was shelved after Prince Hisahito's birth the following year eased immediate concerns.

Increasing the likelihood of a male heir

At the moment, there are only three candidates who could theoretically succeed the 66-year-old Naruhito: his brother, Crown Prince Akishino (60), Prince Hisahito (19), and Naruhito's uncle Prince Hitachi (90).

The latest changes, however, allow male members of former collateral branches of the imperial family to be readmitted. Those branches lost their imperial status after Japan's surrender at the end of World War II and their members have since lived as private citizens.

Under the new law, men from those branches may enter the family through adoption if they are at least 15 years old. The adoptees themselves will not receive succession rights, but their future male-line sons will be eligible for the throne.

Women are still not legally allowed to ascend to the throne.

Opinion polls suggest that the compromise reached by parliament — while endorsed by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi — is unpopular with much of the wider public, who would rather see Emperor Naruhito's daughter inherit the throne. 

A nationwide Mainichi Shimbun poll conducted in June found that 40% of respondents supported both a female emperor and female-line succession, while 33% supported a female emperor but opposed female-line succession. Overall, 73% supported a female emperor, compared with 6% who opposed the idea.

Rethinking imperial succession

This is the first significant revision of the Imperial Household Law since 1947, and it shows "that gender equality is not among the priorities," Sven Saaler of Sophia University in Tokyo said. "A woman on the imperial throne has been pushed even further into the distance by the legal amendment," he told Germany's dpa news agency.

An editorial in Japan's liberal Asahi Shimbun newspaper challenged statements made during parliamentary debate that male-line succession represented an unbroken 2,600-year tradition, arguing that parts of the early imperial lineage are rooted in mythology rather than verifiable history.

European monarchies increasingly adopted gender-neutral succession as women gained political rights after the two world wars, both to reflect equality and secure stable royal succession, added Asahi Shimbun.

Keiko Hongo, an emeritus professor of Japanese medieval history at the University of Tokyo, told Asahi Shimbun that Japan's former women emperors were historically significant, particularly because women were generally excluded from heading samurai families.

Hongo said the current imperial family already had sufficient legitimacy, arguing that succession through women was more natural than restoring the male line through distant relatives.

The president of the Constitutional Democratic Party, Shunichi Mizuoka, questioned the amendment on gender-equality grounds at a press conference and called for an end to male-line male succession.

Conservatives defend tradition

Supporters of the amendment reject the claim that male-line succession discriminates against women.

The conservative Sankei Shimbun supported the amendment, arguing that imperial succession had always followed the male line and was therefore not a matter of gender discrimination.

It warned against abandoning that tradition and said uncertainty remained because Prince Hisahito is the only eligible male heir of his generation.

Akira Momochi, a constitutional law professor at Kokushikan University in Tokyo, told Sankei Shimbun that the reform would help preserve traditional male-line succession.

He argued that there was no immediate need to discuss a female emperor because the succession from Naruhito through Akishino to Hisahito had already been established.

Kenichi Kawamura, a political figure and adviser to the Central Union of Japan-Korea Friendship Associations, said that allowing married female royals to remain in the family was a practical way of ensuring stable imperial succession.

He supported adoption from former imperial branches in principle but said prospective members should ideally receive royal education from childhood.

Kawamura said that, in his personal view, an "Empress Aiko" serving for one reign could be considered as an emergency measure if the male line became unsustainable, without introducing female-line succession.

"The emperor is a ceremonial figure who prays for Japan's enduring prosperity, comparable to the pope, and should remain removed from politics," he told DW.

"I hope the reform will help create a stable imperial family that is respected and cherished by the Japanese people."

Edited by: Keith Walker

entries.title: Meet Andy Burnham, Britain's prime minister
entries.summary: Andy Burnham has been sworn in as the successor to British Prime Minister Keir Starmer. The former Manchester mayor has become a leading figure in the Labour Party and one of the UK's most popular politicians.
entries.content:
United Kingdom

Meet Andy Burnham, Britain's prime minister

Published June 22, 2026last updated July 20, 2026

Andy Burnham has been sworn in as the successor to British Prime Minister Keir Starmer. The former Manchester mayor has become a leading figure in the Labour Party and one of the UK's most popular politicians.

Nearly a month after the resignation of Keir Starmer, former Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, one of the most popular politicians in Britain, has become the country's seventh prime minister in 10 years.

The move was made possible after Burnham won a by-election in the Makerfield constituency by a wide margin. He was then appointed as the leader of the ruling Labour Party on Friday, confirming him as Starmer's successor.

A long political career

"Everyone can feel the country isn't where it should be," 56-year-old Burnham said after his Makerfield election victory, in remarks that already sounded distinctly prime ministerial. "From here on I will give everything I have got to make it so. To ensure the name Makerfield is forever synonymous with bringing about the change this country needs, bringing back something we've lost ― hope ― hope for the future."

Burnham is seen as a leading figure on Labour's moderate-left wing and has decades of experience in both national and regional politics. He first entered Parliament in 2001. Under Prime Minister Tony Blair, he served as a junior minister at the Home Office before Blair's successor, Gordon Brown, appointed him to roles at the Finance Ministry, the Department for Culture and later as health secretary.

Burnham even ran for the Labour leadership twice, in 2010 and 2015. In 2017, he left Parliament to become mayor of Greater Manchester, a region of around 2.8 million people in northern England. He has since won reelection twice, most recently getting nearly two-thirds of the vote.

Among Burnham's most notable achievements in Manchester is the expansion of affordable public transportation. Housing and health care have also been central priorities throughout his time as mayor. He has been critical of Brexit, the UK's exit from the European Union in 2020, and describes himself as an advocate of "pro-business socialism."

During the COVIDpandemic, Burnham locked horns with then-Prime Minister Boris Johnson, demanding greater financial support for businesses and workers affected by lockdown restrictions. The confrontation and his general success in Manchester earned him the nickname "King of the North."

In the past few years, Burnham's main criticism of his Labour Party colleague Starmer has focused on the prime minister's cuts to welfare spending. Which policies Burnham will pursue as prime minister have so far largely remained undefined.

A working-class northerner

Burnham is deeply rooted in the former mining and industrial communities of northern England. Born in 1970 in Aintree, near Liverpool, he grew up in the village of Culcheth with his father working as a technician and his mother as a medical assistant.

While studying English at Cambridge University, Burnham later said he often felt like an outsider. Inspired by the miners' strike of the mid-1980s, he joined the Labour Party at the age of 14.

He's been a lifelong supporter of Everton Football Club. His wife is Dutch, and the couple has three children. Burnham also has a tattoo of the worker bee — a symbol of industry and solidarity, and the emblem of Manchester — on his right upper arm.

Today, Burnham is among the most popular politicians in the United Kingdom, and many supporters see him as Labour's best hope of countering the rise of the right-wing populist Reform UK party of Nigel Farage.

Yet since the Brexit referendum in 2016, the office of prime minister has become somewhat precarious. Burnham will be the seventh person to hold the position since the referendum a decade ago. As he replaces Starmer, he inherits a country still grappling with deep political, economic and social challenges.

Update, July 20, 2026: This article was originally published in German on June 22 and updated on July 20 to reflect Andy Burnham assuming the office of British prime minister.

entries.title: How 'blue dot fever' is affecting concerts
entries.summary: Live music remains popular, but many concerts are struggling with unsold tickets and cancellations. While superstars fill stadiums, smaller venues and acts are facing a crisis.
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

Live music remains popular, but many concerts are struggling with unsold tickets and cancellations. While superstars fill stadiums, smaller venues and acts are facing a crisis.

The term "blue dot fever" has been circulating in the media for some time now. "Blue dots" refer to available seats on US ticket sales platform Ticketmaster. Lots of empty seats right before a concert can spell trouble for both artists and organizers — including cancelled shows. With more and more concerts afflicted by this "illness," is the future of the concert scene uncertain?

The economy of superstars

The situation is quite different for big name stars. Leading musicians like Beyonce, Oasis and Coldplay still easily fill large stadiums.

According to official Pollstar charts, Beyonce had the most successful music tour in 2025, generating over $400 million (Є359 million) in revenue. The Oasis Live '25 tour was one of the biggest box office successes in recent years. Coldplay also remains a strong crowd-pleaser, with many concerts on their current world tour selling out instantly.

Yet all big concerts share a common challenge: rising costs, as points out Johannes Everke from Germany's Federal Association of the Concert and Event Industry: "Due to increasing personnel costs, rising technical costs, energy and inflation, we have soaring production costs in comparison with 2019." As a result, tickets are becoming more expensive, meaning fewer fans can afford them.

However, the impact of these rising costs are not evenly experienced across the board. Stephan Thanscheidt from FKP Scorpio, one of the largest concert and festival promoters in Europe, confirms this. "Several factors are in play here, such as some megastars having huge legions of fans who’d never miss their idols for anything. And if they need to keep an eye on their spending, many people would choose to go for a large concert, perceived to be a major event, over several smaller ones if they need to keep an eye on their spending."

The late US economist Alan B. Krueger called this "the economics of superstars," in which only a few superstars generate most of the revenue.

What about the stars of tomorrow?

Johannes Everke believes the live music market is under significant strain. Typically, organizers rely on revenue generated from large concerts to fund smaller, lesser-known acts. However, this model is at risk of collapsing if the profit margins are reduced.

Everke says that while large ticket sales at mega-events guarantee high revenue, it doesn’t necessarily mean that the promoters are gaining big profits due to ticket prices rising at a slower rate compared to promotion costs.

"The circle of life means supporting smaller bands for a period until they become big enough to start making money. You then take those profits in turn and reinvest in so-called product development by supporting smaller acts once again," Everke told DW.

One prominent example is Ed Sheeran, who, along with others, was supported by FKP Scorpio over the years before becoming a top-selling star. "Many of our current successes began as passion projects, and we couldn't always know whether they would end up being financially successful," Stephan Thanscheidt confirms. "It wouldn’t be possible without cross-financing new ideas or artists we believe in. When we succeed today, we make tomorrow’s successes more likely."

Concerts are no longer promotion tours

Another complicating factor is how the economics of concerts has changed.

Everke explains that in the past, tours served to promote new albums. "You’d play concerts and even if you lost money on them, you’d recoup the costs through album sales. So, labels would sometimes give financial contributions to concert tours." But in the age of streaming, this is no longer the case.

According to Everke, approximately half of artists’ income now comes from live performances. So, the pressure to achieve commercial success through touring is extremely high.

Additionally, many established musicians are having much longer careers now. "They’re all playing an endless series of concerts because record sales alone no longer provide enough for them to make a living," Everke says. This situation leads to a saturated market and intensified competition for audience retention.

Moving away from touring?

Some megastars have shifted away from touring and are now establishing "residencies." These are series of concerts in which artists perform at the same venue over an extended period of time.

One major example of this is Shakira, who is set to play a series of 12 concerts in the second half of 2026 in Madrid. Demand has been so high that additional dates have been announced on more than one occasion. The performances will take place in the "Estadio Shakira," a built-for-purpose stadium.

Harry Styles is another such example: In June, he played 12 concerts in London's Wembley Stadium.

Johannes Everke does not see this being a sustainable model for the future of the market though, as only very few artists are able to attract fans from all over Europe, or across continents.

Why solidarity is needed — the British example

Johannes Everke remains optimistic despite the challenges and warning signals. Live music events are in demand as never before, and the market is growing.

According to the GEMA organization, around 70 million people attended concerts featuring popular music (mostly pop and rock) in Germany in 2024, which is more than before the COVID-19 pandemic.

Even though a select few superstars will continue to account for the majority of the market’s revenue in the near future, Everke believes that the market will still expand to a broader audience. He remains certain that "people always want to hear something new and will always be wanting to hear new music and new trends."

Everke maintains that it is important to continue supporting smaller events. He cites a positive example from the British LIVE Trust model — at many large concerts, one pound per ticket is voluntarily contributed to a fund that supports small venues and the grassroots music scene.

Harry Styles supported this effort during his London residency, which Everke says is a "fantastic sign of solidarity within the industry."

Perhaps the many "blue dots" are not a sign of waning interest in live music, but instead show a market in transition. The challenge now is to preserve and nurture smaller venues and artists.

This article was originally written in German.

entries.title: DRC's neighbors boost preparedness as Ebola cases surge
entries.summary: Uganda, Rwanda and Zambia are strengthening border screening, disease surveillance and emergency response measures. The growing Ebola outbreak in the DRC is raising concerns about cross-border spread.
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

Uganda, Rwanda and Zambia are strengthening border screening, disease surveillance and emergency response measures. The growing Ebola outbreak in the DRC is raising concerns about cross-border spread.

Countries bordering the Democratic Republic of Congo have stepped up screening at border crossings, trained frontl-ine health workers and activated emergency plans to reduce the risk of imported cases as the DRC battles a growing Ebola outbreak.

On Monday the DRC's Health Ministry reported that at least 930 people have died due to the illness. The DRC has recorded 2,344 cases of Bundibugyo, the virus behind the current outbreak, which the government declared on May 15. The World Health Organization (WHO) recently warned that the number of cases could be up to four times higher than what is known.

In Zambia, authorities have stepped up screening at points of entry despite recording no confirmed Ebola cases. Dr. Naeem Dalal, assistant director for preparedness and response at the Zambia National Public Health Institute (ZNPHI), told DW that prevention measures had been reinforced nationwide.

"Infection prevention and control is currently being activated, where a lot of disinfection will be taking place, and face masks and washing of hands with soap and water will become mandatory," Dalal said.

He said provincial public health emergency response centers had been activated to coordinate a rapid response should suspected cases be detected.

Uganda, which has successfully contained previous Ebola outbreaks, has also increased surveillance along its border with the DRC. Speaking on Uganda National Television, Dr. Beda Senkware, of Kabale Referral Hospital, said three western districts of the country remained particularly vulnerable.

"The risk of getting this disease, Ebola, is very high. We have three districts that border the DRC — Kisoro, Kanungu and Rukungiri. These three districts have been put at the highest risk of getting Ebola," Senkware said.

Rwanda has likewise strengthened health screening at border crossings, expanded disease surveillance and prepared rapid response teams to investigate suspected cases.

East African Community's response to Ebola

Regional cooperation is also increasing. The East African Community (EAC) has agreed on a coordinated response to improve preparedness and reduce the risk of cross-border transmission.

EAC Secretary General Stephen Mbundi said partner states were working with the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC), the WHO and other partners to expand surveillance, laboratory testing and emergency response capacity.

"We are mobilizing regional mechanisms and working closely with Partner States, Africa CDC, WHO and development partners to strengthen surveillance, laboratory diagnosis, infection prevention and control, risk communication and rapid response capacities," Mbundi said.

The heightened preparedness follows a warning from the WHO that the outbreak may be significantly larger than official figures indicate because many infections are going undetected in conflict-affected areas of eastern DRC.

Speaking during a virtual news conference in Geneva, WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said the outbreak had become "the third-largest Ebola outbreak on record" and had "expanded faster than any previous outbreak" over the past month.

Tedros also called on international donors to help close a funding gap of more than $400 million (€344 million) for the joint WHO-Africa CDC preparedness-and-response plan.

The outbreak is centered in eastern DRC, where conflict, insecurity and population displacement have complicated efforts to identify cases, trace contacts and contain the virus. The DRC has experienced several Ebola outbreaks since the virus was first identified there in 1976, but health experts say insecurity has made the current outbreak especially difficult to contain. There is currently no known vaccine for Bundibugyo.

Africa CDC urges early Ebola detection

Africa CDC Director-General Jean Kaseya urged neighboring countries to strengthen surveillance and laboratory capacity to improve early detection.

"This outbreak started in Ituri. Now it's moved to Uganda and to other parts of the DRC. That is putting countries neighboring the DRC at risk," Kaseya said, adding that timely detection remains critical to preventing wider transmission.

"If countries do not increase capacity to detect, they are really at risk," Kaseya said. "This is the message we are giving to countries: If you are near the DRC, please increase your capacity to detect."

Professor Aileen Marty, an infectious disease specialist at Florida International University, said the ongoing violence continues to undermine containment efforts.

"The cases have continued to rise because it's a highly inflamed area where there has been ongoing conflict for over a decade," Marty said.

"A few of our treatment centers have been burnt down," she said. "That discourages health workers from going there to manage patients and increases the number of people who travel elsewhere while infected. That is also making it extraordinarily challenging."

Marty said the situation underscored the importance of preparedness in neighboring countries, where surveillance, rapid diagnosis and coordinated public health responses remain the best defense against regional spread.

Apart from Uganda, no neighboring country has confirmed a case linked to the outbreak, but health officials say vigilance remains essential until transmission in the DRC is brought under control. They warn that sustained investment in surveillance, laboratory capacity and regional cooperation will be critical to preventing the virus from further spreading across borders.

Edited by: Chrispin Mwakideu

entries.title: Germany news: Resistance to Nazi terror commemorated
entries.summary: Monday marks 82 years since "Operation Valkyrie," the failed attempt on Adolf Hitler's life in 1944. Meanwhile, Foreign Minister Wadephul is heading to Africa on official business. Follow DW.
entries.content:
Germany

Germany news: Resistance to Nazi terror commemorated

Published July 20, 2026last updated July 20, 2026

Monday marks 82 years since "Operation Valkyrie," the failed attempt on Adolf Hitler's life in 1944. Meanwhile, Foreign Minister Wadephul is heading to Africa on official business. Follow DW.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • July 20, Germany commemorates the resistance to the Nazi reign of terror in Berlin in 1944
  • Germany's top diplomat begins 3-nation Africa visit that takes in Mauritania, Nigeria and South Africa

Below you can read some of the top stories coming out of Germany on Monday, July 20:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Cost of renting houses in Germany up 4%

Cost of renting houses in Germany up 4%

The cost of rent has risen 4% year-on year in the second quarter, according to figures from the German Economic Institute (IW).

The IW said that rent continued a significant nationwide rise as it provided statistics on rental prices and stagnating purchase prices.

"Rents rose particularly sharply in Cologne (+7.9%), followed by Hamburg (+5.5%) and Leipzig (+5.4%)," the IW said in a new report.

The IW said that the supply of rental apartments remained below the level seen in 2022 in most major cities.

It added that the rental market was battling with a shortage of available units with dramatic declines seen in a number of cities.

"In Hamburg, the number of rental listings has more than halved, by 57%. Frankfurt am Main has seen a 43% decrease in listings, and Leipzig a 40% drop," the IW said.

The exception was Berlin, where there was a 43% increase when compared to 2022.

Skip next section WATCH: Why Germany struggles to recruit women soldiers

WATCH: Why Germany struggles to recruit women soldiers

As Germany expands its military to meet NATO commitments, attracting more women remains a challenge. Concerns range from military culture to family life and representation.

Skip next section Teen charged after allegedly planning attack on synagogue

Teen charged after allegedly planning attack on synagogue

German prosecutors have charged a 15-year-old with preparing a serious act of violence endangering the state. The charge relates to a pyrotechnic attack on a synagogue and was filed at the Augsburg District Court.

Authorities say the suspect stockpiled a large amount of pyrotechnic materials that was aimed at carrying out an attack on a synagogue.

The suspect is also alleged to have sympathized with the so-called"Islamic State" and also allegedly was involved in an explosion in October 2025, in which he blew off two of his fingers.

The teenager is in pre-trial detention and his defense lawyer has rejected the allegation of a planned attack.

Skip next section German's Wadephul heads to Africa for three-nation visit

German's Wadephul heads to Africa for three-nation visit

German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul travels to Africa on Monday.

The first destination will be Mauritania followed by Nigeria and then South Africa. 

"Especially in times of global geopolitical upheaval, reliable cooperation with African partners is crucial. For Germany and Europe, this is about more than the obvious interest in a stable and prosperous neighboring continent," Wadephul said in comments before leaving.

"It's about making offers to one another. That's why I'm visiting key African countries for the third time in just a few months," Wadephul said.

The German diplomat is being accompanied by a business delegation, with the purpose of the visit trip also aimed at identifying opportunities for German companies and promoting private investment to support economic growth.

Skip next section Germany commemorates resistance to Nazi terror in Berlin

Germany commemorates resistance to Nazi terror in Berlin

Commemoration events will be taking place in Berlin to remember those who stood up to Nationalist Socialist terror, with ceremonies being held at historic sites of the failed coup of 20 July 1944.

On this day 82 years ago, Colonel Claus von Stauffenberg attempted to assassinate Adolf Hitler by planting a bomb at the Wolf's Lair headquarters. The device exploded, but failed to kill the Nazi leader.

Von Stauffenberg and three others involved in the plot to kill Hitler were summarily executed in the courtyard of the Bendler Block complex, the former headquarters of the Army High Command.

The site is one of several places of historical significance linked to the daring attempt on Hitler's life. Each year ceremonies are held and wreaths are laid to remember those who died resisting the Nationalist Socialist reign of terror in Berlin.

One of the sites is the Plötzensee Memorial Center complex, which from 1933 and 1945 served as a prison and also a place of execution under the Nazi regime. According to the German government 2,800 people were executed there, among them, many opponents of the Nazis.

In 1953, a monument was created in the Bendler block to "to keep alive the memory of the men and women who took part in the resistance to National Socialism and aid the Germans in the necessary process of coming to terms with this part of their history," the Domestic Protocol Office says on its website.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

Guten Morgen from the DW newsroom here in Bonn.

Today, Germany is remembering those who stood up to the Nazi regime with commemoration events marking 82 years since the failed coup attempt in 1944.

The actions of Colonel Claus von Stauffenberg in "Operation Valkyrie" would result in his summary execution, but also demonstrate the lengths some were willing to go to remove Adolf Hitler.

Meanwhile, Germany's top diplomat heads to Africa as Berlin seeks to strengthen ties. Wadephul will be visiting Mauritania, Nigeria and South Africa on his whistle-stop visit.

Stay with us for these stories and more as we bring you all the latest from across Germany!

entries.title: Taking stock of 2026 World Cup: Football's biggest show yet
entries.summary: A worthy world champion, a call from the US president, security concerns and a few pleasant surprises: The biggest World Cup ever is now over. So what will we remember about the 2026 World Cup?
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

A worthy world champion, a call from the US president, security concerns and a few pleasant surprises: The biggest World Cup ever is now over. So what will we remember about the 2026 World Cup?

Football didn't have it easy at the 2026 World Cup, co-hosted by the United States, Mexico and Canada. 

There were questionable decisions by FIFA, the sport's governing body, as well as a lot of spectacle and a final in which only one team actually played football.

After a total of 104 games in this biggest-ever World Cup, Spain were deservedly crowned champions, and, for the first time, a country's men's and women's teams are simultaneously world champions.

FIFA President Gianni Infantino described the tournament as the "greatest sporting, social and cultural event in humanity." 

US President Donald Trump congratulated FIFA on what he called "one of the all-time greatest sporting events in history."

But was it really? Here's what will be remembered.

How did viewers receive the biggest Cup yet?

Fears of empty stadiums — especially in the United States, where international football isn't the No. 1 sport — proved unfounded. According to FIFA, 6,810,966 fans attended the tournament, the first to be co-hosted by three countries. This set a new record for World Cup attendance.

Given that the schedule featured 104 matches, instead of 64 at previous tournaments, record attendance is no surprise. The average match attendance was about 65,000, and, according to FIFA, 99.7% of all available seats were filled. However, many empty seats were visible in stadiums, especially during the group stage.

Hydration breaks draw criticism

Fans criticized the newly introduced hydration breaks. Most coaches, however, supported the breaks — or, at least, did not complain about them.

Uruguay’s now-former coach, Marcelo Bielsa, on the other hand, criticized the additional opportunity to influence the game.

"Playing four halves instead of two changes the culturally shaped perception of football," he said. "These breaks add nothing to the game, but take a lot away from it."

The 2026 World Cup's many surprises

World Cup newcomer Cape Verde caused quite a stir. The small African nation held eventual world champions Spain to a 0-0 draw and narrowly lost to defending champions Argentina in extra time in the round of 32.

The Democratic Republic of Congo celebrated a 1-1 draw against Portugal and its superstar, Cristiano Ronaldo. Paraguay knocked Germany out of the tournament in the round of 32. Curacao, the smallest World Cup participant, made history by scoring their first-ever World Cup goal against Germany and earned their first-ever World Cup point against Ecuador.

Price madness at World Cup

This World Cup was expensive. Fans had to dig deep into their pockets for tickets, hotels, parking fees and flights. Prices inside the stadiums caused additional frustration. In the United States, beer prices could top $20 (€17.50), French fries ran €17, and even a bottle of water fetched €7.

In Mexico, too, food and drinks were significantly more expensive than usual. At the Azteca Stadium, where the opening match took place, a beer cost the equivalent of about €15.50 — almost as much as the country's minimum daily wage.

Donald Trump's World Cup

There was widespread concern that Trump might use the World Cup to advance his political agenda. After all, the US president had repeatedly declared the tournament to be his personal prestige project. Yet Trump didn’t show up at the stadium until the final and largely kept a low profile during the tournament.

Nevertheless, the 80-year-old sparked the biggest scandal of the tournament. After US forward Folarin Balogun was shown a red card, Trump successfully lobbied FInfantino to review the automatic ban for the next game.

Balogun was allowed to play in the round-of-16 match against Belgium, but couldn't stop the United States from losing 4-1.

"The current FIFA president has bowed to Donald Trump," said Sepp Blatter, Infantino's predecessor as FIFA boss. However, the criticism is unlikely to jeopardize Infantino's reelection as FIFA president should he seek the post a third time in March 2027.

World Cup incidents

The war in the Middle East and unrest in Mexico had raised significant security concerns in the run-up to the tournament. Though the games largely went off without a hitch in Canada and the United States, there were several incidents in Mexico. Fans stormed an already overcrowded fan zone in Guadalajara, in another incident a car plowed into a crowd following a national team win, and several people died during victory celebrations in Mexico City.

Like numerous fans, particularly from African countries, Somali referee Omar Artan was denied entry into the United States despite having a valid visa, with US authorities alleging that he had ties to a terror organization in his home country. Artan is expected to receive his salary for the World Cup.

The World Cup human rights situation

Many human rights organizations have given FIFA an extremely poor rating.

"This World Cup has been played against a backdrop of the US government’s abusive immigration crackdown," said Minky Worden of Human Rights Watch.

She said there had been a "failure by FIFA to uphold its own human rights standards."

This seems to contradict FIFA's claims of the "most inclusive World Cup in history."

"FIFA had promised that everyone could feel safe. The truth is different. ICE, for example, has doubled the number of arrests," said Daniel Norona of Amnesty International USA.

Ronan Evain, of the fan organization "Football Supporters Europe," criticized the opaque process for issuing visas to fans, particularly those from non-European countries.

"Our observations have revealed little or no evidence that ticket holders from Africa and Asia actually received visas to enter the US," Evain said.

What's next after the World Cup?

Infantino has discussed his desire to further expand the World Cup, from 48 to 64 teams. Instead of the current 104 matches, there would then be 128.

"That is definitely an issue that will be examined after this World Cup and discussed within the committees," Infantino told the Swiss broadcaster blue Sport.

Despite the widespread criticism of Infantino, his position as FIFA president is not in jeopardy. On the contrary: More games mean more money that he can distribute to the member nations.

If reelected in March, Infantino would be in charge until 2031, a year after the next World Cup, which is to be spread over three continents. A total of three games will be played in Uruguay, Paraguay and Argentina before the tournament moves to Spain, Portugal and Morocco.

This article was originally published in German.

entries.title: UK: Andy Burnham takes office as seventh PM in 10 years
entries.summary: After securing the leadership of the ruling Labour Party last week, Burnham has been invited to form a government by King Charles III. He promised both stability and major reforms in his first speech.
entries.content:
United Kingdom

UK: Andy Burnham takes office as seventh PM in 10 years

July 20, 2026

After securing the leadership of the ruling Labour Party last week, Burnham has been invited to form a government by King Charles III. He promised both stability and major reforms in his first speech.

Newly-minted Labour Party leader Andy Burnham has met with the UK's King Charles III on Monday, the traditional final step in becoming the country's head of government.

Burnham is the seventh British prime minister in the last 10 years since the UK voted to leave the European Union.

His predecessor Keir Starmer announced that he was stepping aside in June after several Cabinet ministers resigned amid plummeting poll numbers and questions about his leadership.

Former mayor of Greater Manchester, Burnham managed to win a risky special election in June in order to re-enter parliament.

Burnham promises to 'bring hope back' in first speech

Speaking for the first time outside his new home of 10 Downing Street, Burnham said that he was "acutely aware" of being the seventh prime minister in ten years, and promised to "make politics work better" than it has since the Brexit vote.

"I know people at home are fed up with politics, I hear you…we have not been good enough, and we need to be better," the PM said, before promising to bring about "the biggest changes in the last 40 years."

He then took aim at the privatization of public services and de-industrialization of Britain in the 1980s, without mentioning the names of any of his predecessors. Burnham said he would present a 10-year plan for a new economic and political model by the end of the year.

Adding that he was aware that the UK is facing a cost of living crisis right now, he promised to put forward a few proposals for easing the situation as early as tomorrow.

The Labour leader then said his very first concern was to "end rough sleeping in this country" and alleviate the housing crisis, before promising to "bring back hope" to all UK residents.

European leaders congratulate Burnham

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz offered his congratulations to Burnham in a social media message, adding that "Germany are the United Kingdom are close partners. I look forward to working together."

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen wrote on X that: "I look forward to working with you to strengthen the EU-UK partnership. For the security of our shared continent. And the prosperity of people on both sides of the Channel."

Also writing on X, France's Emmanuel Macron said that "in the face of the challenges that unite us, I hope that we will deepen our relationship in the service of our peoples and give new momentum to the partnership between the United Kingdom and the European Union."

Ukrainian President Volodomyr Zelenskyy, whom Burnham has vowed to support, said he was "looking forward to our conversation later today," adding that the "foundation of our bilateral relations is stronger than ever, and we will continue to make each other stronger."

Starmer formally resigns

For Burnham to officially become prime minister, Keir Starmer had to first formally tender his resignation to King Charles III.

In prepared remarks as he left Downing Street, the outgoing leader highlighted his role in taking Labour from slumping poll numbers to a "landslide victory" two years ago.

"Britain is now stronger and fairer than it was two years ago," he said, claiming a better economy, shorter NHS wait times, and decreasing immigration.

He wished his successor "every success" and offered his full support.

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Guyana: Dozens feared dead after ferry capsizes
entries.summary: Rescuers have been able to save 67 passengers so far. It is unknown exactly how many people were on board as authorities said the passengers' list is inaccurate.
entries.content:
Latin America

Guyana: Dozens feared dead after ferry capsizes

July 20, 2026

Rescuers have been able to save 67 passengers so far. It is unknown exactly how many people were on board as authorities said the passengers' list is inaccurate.

Several people were feared dead after a ferry carrying more than 100 people capsized off the coast of Guyana, authorities said.

The ferry MV Barima left Guyana at the northeast corner of South America on Saturday afternoon, carrying at least 133 people for a trip from the capital Georgetown to Port Kaituma.

Public Works Minister Juan Edghill said that the list of people on board was not "a true reflection of the occupants on board," asking family members and loved ones to reach out to officials to create an official tally of those missing.

The inaccuracy in the list of passengers has created an additional challenge as authorities do not know how many people they are searching for.

Rescue operations underway

The rescue operations were launched after air traffic control received a distress call from the ferry around 11 p.m. local time on Saturday.

Rescue teams have so far saved 67 people. The rescued victims include 41 men, 11 women, six boys and nine girls, Prime Minister Mark Phillips said in a statement Sunday.

"The families of those rescued, as well as the relatives of all recorded passengers and crew members, have been engaged," Phillips said, adding that "the captain and mate of the vessel, who were among those rescued, have returned to the area to assist with the search and rescue operation."

However, no survivors have been found since Sunday afternoon.

"Search and rescue is still in progress," Public Works Minister ‌Edghill said. "So to the families that are out there wondering about their loved ones, we are doing everything that is humanly possible to find them."

What do we know about the cause of the incident?

Prime Minister Mark Phillips said investigators are looking into the incident, adding that two crew members were found to have tested positive for cannabis.

Phillips said that the vessel, which had had maintenance in 2024 and was due for an inspection later this year, reported a cargo weight of 268 tons out of a 284-ton-capacity.

It was licensed to carry more than 300 passengers and it was equipped with 250 life jackets, six inflatable life rafts and two rigid life rafts, according to the prime minister.

"Let me be absolutely clear: where negligence, misconduct or criminal wrongdoing is established, those responsible will face the full force of the law," Phillips said in a Sunday evening press conference.

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.
